entries.text: ... Night and Day which L. has spent the past two mornings and evenings in reading. I own that his verdict, finally pronounced this morning, gives me immense pleasure: how far one should discount it, I don't know. In my own opinion N. & D. is a much more mature and finished and satisfactory book than The Voyage Out; as it has reason to be. I suppose I lay myself open to the charge of niggling with emotions that don't really matter. I certainly don't anticipate even two editions. And yet I can't help thinking that, English fiction being what it is, I compare for originality and sincerity rather well with most of the modern. L. finds the philosophy very melancholy. It too much agrees with what he was saying yesterday. Yet, if one is to deal with people on a large scale and say what one thinks, how can one avoid melancholy? I don't admit to being hopeless though: only the spectacle is a profoundly strange one; and as the current answers don't do, one has to grope for a new one, and the process of discarding the old, when one is by no means certain what to put in their place, is a sad one. Still, if you think of it, what answers do Arnold Bennett or Thackeray, for instance, suggest? Happy ones—satisfactory solutions—answers one would accept, if one had the least respect for one's soul? Now I have done my last odious piece of typewriting, and when I have scribbled this page, I shall write and suggest Monday as the day for coming up to lunch with Gerald. * I don't suppose I've ever enjoyed any writing so much as I did the last half of Night and Day. Indeed, no part of it taxed me as The Voyage Out did; and if one's own ease and interest promise anything good, I should have hopes that some people, at least, will find it a pleasure. I wonder if I shall ever be able to read it again? Is the time coming when I can endure to read my own writing in print without blushing—shivering and wishing to take cover?

It is an utterly corrupt society I have just remarked, speaking in the person of Elvira Pargiter, and I will take nothing that it can give me etc. etc.: Now, as Virginia Woolf, I have to write—oh dear me what a bore—to the Vice Chancellor of Manchester University and say that I refuse to be made a Doctor of Letters. And to Lady Simon, who has been urgent in the matter and asks us to stay. Lord knows how I'm to put Elvira's language into polite journalese. What an odd coincidence! that real life should provide precisely the situation I was writing about. I hardly know which I am, or where: Virginia or Elvira: in the Pargiters or outside. We dined with Susan Lawrence two nights ago. A Mrs. Stocks of Manchester University was there. How delighted my husband will be to give you your degree in July! she began. And had rattled off a great deal about the delight of Manchester in seeing me hon-oured, before I had to pluck up courage and say: "But I won't take it." After that there was a general argument, with the Nevinsons, (Evelyn Sharp) Susan Lawrence etc. They all said they would take a degree from a University though not an honour from the state. They made me feel a little silly, priggish and perhaps extreme: but only superficially. Nothing would induce me to connive at all that humbug. Nor would it give me, even illicitly, any pleasure. I really believe that Nessa and I—she went with me and used my arguments about the silliness of honours for women—are without the publicity sense. Now for the polite letters. Dear Vice Chancellor—

And this morning, in spite of being in a rage, I wrote the whole of that d——d chapter again, in a spasm of desperation and, I think, got it right, by breaking up, the use of thought skipping and parentheses. Anyhow that's the hang of it. And I cut from 20 to 30 pages.

I see I am becoming a regular diariser. The reason is that I cannot make the transition from Pargiters to Dante without some bridge. And this cools my mind. I am rather worried about the raid chapter: afraid if I compress and worry that I shall spoil. Never mind. Forge ahead and see what comes next.

Yesterday we went to the Tower, which is an impressive murderous bloody grey raven haunted military barrack prison dungeon place; like the prison of English splendour; the reformatory at the back of history; where we shot and tortured and imprisoned. Prisoners scratched their names, very beautifully, on the walls. And the crown jewels blazed, very tawdry, and there were the orders, like Spinks or a Regent Street jewellers. And we watched the Scots Guards drill: and an officer doing a kind of tiger pace up and down—a wax faced barbers block officer trained to a certain impassive balancing. The sergeant major barked and swore. All in a hoarse bark: the men stamped and wheeled like—machines: then the officer also barked: all precise, inhuman, showing off. A degrading, stupefying sight. But in keeping with the grey walls, the cobbles, the executioner's block. People sitting on the river bank among old cannon. Steps etc. very romantic: a dungeon like feeling.

No, I am not going to titivate Gibbon—that is condense by a thousand words. Too much screw needed, and my brain unstrung. Merely scribbling here: over a log fire, on a cold but bright Easter morning; sudden shafts of sun, a scatter of snow on the hills early; sudden storms, ink black, octopus pouring, coming up; and the rooks fidgeting and pecking on the elm trees. As for the beauty, as I always say when I walk the terrace after breakfast, too much for one pair of eyes. Enough to float a whole population in happiness, if only they would look. Curiously a combination, this garden, with the church, and the cross of the church black against Asheham Hill. That is all the elements of the English brought together, accidentally. We came down on Thursday, packed in the rush in London; cars spinning all along the roads: yesterday at last perfect freedom from telephones and reviews, and no one rang up. I began Lord Ormont and his Aminta and found it so rich, so knotted, so alive, and muscular after the pale little fiction I'm used to, that, alas, it made me wish to write fiction again. Meredith underrated. I like his effort to escape plain prose. And he had humour and some insight too—more than they allow him now. Also Gibbon. And so I'm well fitted out; but can't write more than this without the old tightening and throbbing at the back of the head.

Theosophical lectures by Dr. Rudolf Steiner Berlin. Rhetorical effect: Comfortable discussion of the objections of opponents, the listener is astonished at this strong opposition, further elaboration and praise of these objections, the listener begins to worry, complete immersion in these objections as if there were nothing else, the listener now considers a refutation altogether impossible and is more than satisfied with a cursory description of the possibility of a defense.

 This rhetorical effect is consistent, moreover, with the rule of the devotional mood.—Constant looking at the palm of the held-out hand.—Omission of the period. In general the spoken sentence begins with its initial capital letter from the orator, bends in its course as far as it can out to the listeners and returns with the period to the orator. But if the period is omitted, then the sentence, no longer held back, blows directly on the listener with full breath.

 previously lecture Loos and Kraus.

 In Western European stories, as soon as they want to include even a few groups of Jews, we are now almost accustomed to immediately seeking and finding under or over the depiction the solution to the Jewish question too. Yet in Jüdinnen such a solution is not shown indeed not even presumed, for those very characters who occupy themselves with such questions stand farther away from the center of the story, at a point where events are already spinning more rapidly, so that, even though we can still observe them closely, we no longer find an opportunity to receive from them calm information about their efforts. Without further ado we recognize in this a deficiency of the story and feel all the more entitled to such a criticism as today since the existence of Zionism the possibilities for a solution are so clearly arrayed around the Jewish problem that in the end the writer would have had to take only a few steps to find the possibility for a solution appropriate to his story.

 But this deficiency arises from still another one. Jüdinnen lacks the non-Jewish spectators, the respectable opposing people, who in other stories lure the Jewishness out so that it advances against them, is astonished, afflicted with doubt, envy horror and finally, finally inspired with self-confidence, but in any case can draw itself up to its full height only opposite them. We demand just that, we don’t accept any other dissolution of Jewish masses. And we invoke this feeling not only in this case, it is general in at least one respect. So too do lizards starting at our steps on a footpath in Italy delight us immensely we keep wanting to bend down, but if we see them at a dealer’s crawling in disorder by the hundreds in the large jars otherwise used for pickling cucumbers, then we don’t know how to orient ourselves

 Both deficiencies unite into a third. “Jüdinnen” can do without that young man at the forefront who within his story usually seizes the best and leads them in a beautiful radial direction to the boundaries of the Jewish circle. That is just what we cannot grasp, that the story can do without this young man, here we sense a flaw more than we see it.

 Today is your birthday, but I’m not even sending you the usual book, for it would be nothing but pretense; at bottom I’m not even capable of giving you a book. I’m writing only because I so need to be close to you for a moment today and be it only with this card, and I’ve begun with the complaint so that you recognize me immediately.

 In Western European stories, as soon as they want to include even a few groups of Jews, we are now almost accustomed to immediately seeking and finding somewhere under or over the depiction the solution to the Jewish question too. In “Jüdinnen,” however, such a solution is not shown, indeed not even presumed, for those very characters who concern themselves with such questions stand farther away from the center of the story, at a point where events are already spinning more rapidly, so that, even though we can still observe them closely, we no longer find an opportunity to receive from them calm information about their efforts. Without further ado we recognize in this a deficiency of the story and feel all the more entitled to such a criticism as today since the existence of Zionism the possibilities for a solution stand so clearly arrayed around the Jewish problem that in the end the writer would have had to take only a few steps to find the possibility for a solution appropriate to his story.

 It had by now become a habit of the four friends Robert Samuel, Max and Franz to use their short vacation every summer or autumn for a trip together. During the rest of the year their friendship consisted mostly in the fact that all four of them liked to meet one evening a week usually at Samuel’s who as the wealthiest had a spacious room, to tell each other various things while drinking a moderate amount of beer. At midnight when they parted they were never finished with the telling, for Robert was secretary of an association, Samuel an employee of a commercial office, Max a civil servant and Franz an official in a bank, so that almost everything one of them had experienced in his profession during the week was unknown to the other three and told to them quickly but also incomprehensible to them without elaborate explanation. But above all the diversity of these professions meant that each of them was forced to describe his profession to the others again and again, for the others being only weak people after all didn’t understand these descriptions thoroughly enough but for that very reason and also out of good friendship demanded them again and again. Stories about women, on the other hand, were rarely related, for even if Samuel personally would have enjoyed them, he took care not to demand that the conversation adapt itself to his needs, while the old maid who fetched the beer often appeared to him as an admonition. But they laughed so much on these evenings that Max said on the way home that this perpetual laughter was actually regrettable, because it made one forget all the serious matters of which, after all, everyone had enough to bear at the moment. While one was laughing one thought there would still be time enough for seriousness. But that wasn’t correct, for seriousness naturally made greater demands on a person and it was clear, after all, that in the company of friends one was also capable of satisfying greater demands than alone. One should laugh in the office, because there one wouldn’t achieve anything more. This opinion was directed at Robert, who worked a great deal in his in the old art association that he was rejuvenating and at the same time noticed the funniest things with which he entertained his friends. As soon as he began his friends left their seats, stood around him or sat down on the table and laughed especially Max and Franz with such abandon that Samuel carried all the glasses over to a little side table. When they were tired of telling stories Max sat down at the piano with suddenly renewed strength and played, while Robert and Samuel sat beside him on the little bench, Franz on the other hand who understood nothing of music, remained alone at the table looking through Samuel’s picture postcard collection or reading the newspaper. When the evenings grew warmer and the window could be left open, all four might come to the window and with their hands behind their backs look down into the street without letting the traffic, light though it was, divert them from their conversation. Only now and then one of them went back to the table to take a sip or pointed at the curly hair of two girls sitting down below in front of their wine tavern or at the moon, which easily surprised them or Max described what he was telling with outspread fingers out in the air, reaching over his neighbor’s shoulder, until finally Franz said it was cool, they should close the window. In summer they sometimes met in a public garden, sat down at a table at the very edge, where it was darker, drank to each other and with their heads together in conversation hardly noticed the distant brass band. Arm in arm, in step with each other, they then walked home through the park. The two at the ends twirled their canes or struck the bushes, Robert [One thinks one describes him correctly, but it is only approximated and is corrected by the diary.] invited them to sing, but then sang by himself well enough for four, making the other in the middle feel especially safe and secure. One such evening Franz, pressing his two neighbors more closely to him, said it was so nice to be together that he couldn’t understand why they met only once a week, when it would certainly be easy to arrange to see each other, if not often, then at least twice a week why not twice instead. All were in favor of it, even the fourth one who from the outside had understood Franz’s soft speaking only indistinctly. Such a pleasure would certainly be worth the slight trouble it would now and then cause one of them. It seemed to Franz as if, in punishment for speaking unbidden for all of them, his voice had grown hollow. But he didn’t desist. And if one of them ever really couldn’t come, then it would simply be his loss and he could be consoled the next time, but was that any reason for the others to have to forgo each other, weren’t three enough for each other and if necessary even two. Of course, of course said everyone. Samuel detached himself from the end and walked just ahead of the 3 others, because in that way they were closer to each other. But then, on second thought, this seemed to him not to be the case and he preferred to link arms with them. Robert made a proposal: We meet every week and study Italian. We’re determined to learn Italian for last year already we saw in the little piece of Italy where we were that our Italian suffices only to ask the way when we got lost you remember among the vineyard walls of the Campagna. And even for that it sufficed only with great effort on the part of those we asked. So we must study if we want to go to Italy again this year. There’s no way around it. And isn’t it best, then, to study together? No said Max we won’t learn anything together. I’m just as certain of that as you Sam are in favor of studying together. Indeed I am said Samuel. We’ll definitely learn very well together, I always regret only that we weren’t together already in school. Do you realize that we’ve known each other for only 2 years. He leaned forward to see all 3. They had slowed their pace and loosened their arms. But we haven’t learned anything together yet said Franz. I like it very much that way. I don’t want to study anything at all. But if we must learn Italian, then it’s better for each of us to learn it for himself. I don’t understand that said Samuel. First you want us to meet every week, then you don’t want it. “Nonsense said Max, Franz and I just don’t want our togetherness to be disturbed by studying or our studying by togetherness nothing else.” Quite right said Franz. There isn’t much time left anyhow said Max it’s June now and in September we want to leave. That’s exactly why I want us to study together said Robert, looking wide-eyed at the two who were against him. His neck in particular grew limber when someone contradicted him.

 It probably lies in the nature of friendship and follows it like a shadow—one will welcome it, the other regret it, the third not even notice it
